name: Abdul Muhaimin Abdul Malik
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Abdul Muhaimin Abdul Malik,

As part of the Workers' Party team representing Sengkang GRC, you work alongside colleagues who have already engaged with digital rights and data protection questions in Parliament. I write to ensure that this particular issue reaches your constituency office as well, and to ask whether your team might find it useful as a case study in what the PDPA promises in theory versus what it delivers in practice.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had allowed public segmentation of cases by obligation type. After the redesign, the segmentation is gone, and the zero Access Obligation breach finding, visible in the data before the change, became inaccessible to most members of the public. The data was made inconvenient rather than deleted.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal remains the most direct analogy. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access their personal data. Section 24 imposes a protection obligation. PDPC has enforced s.24 actively across dozens of cases, including against major institutions. It has never published a breach finding under s.21 in 374 published enforcement decisions. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding in the record.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains accessible to any resident who wants to verify it. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would your constituency office find it useful to have this documented gap on record, should a resident raise it with your team?

Four sites to verify:
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) — full account of the documented pattern, the rebuilt enforcement filter, and the primary record
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) — the rebuilt enforcement matrix showing 0 Access Obligation breach findings across 374 published decisions
- [pdpc.gov.sg/enforcement-decisions](https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/enforcement-decisions?type=Commission%27s+Decisions&page=1&sort=latest) — the official PDPC published-decisions page, filterable by obligation type
- [Hansard: Written Answer 19596](https://sprs.parl.gov.sg/search/#/sprs3topic?reportid=written-answer-19596) — Minister Josephine Teo's Parliamentary assurance of 22 September 2025 on data preservation + criminal penalties for intentional concealment

Attachments:
- BreachBreakdown.jpg (PDPC breach-by-obligation chart: 204 Protection vs 0 Access Obligation)
- entry-gate-overlay.jpg (pdpaaccessrights.sg entry layover)
- pdpa-masking-42.png (PDPC Advisory Guidelines page 42, para 4.59: blurred/pixelated masking may not be foolproof)
- hansard.jpg (Parliament Written Answer 19596, 22 September 2025: assurance on preservation + criminal penalties)

Yours sincerely,
Lim Zirui (Ray Lim)

name: Alex Yam Ziming
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Mayor Alex Yam Ziming,

As Mayor of North West District, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

- [pdpc.gov.sg/enforcement-decisions](https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/enforcement-decisions?type=Commission%27s+Decisions&page=1&sort=latest) — the official PDPC published-decisions page, filterable by obligation type
- [Hansard: Written Answer 19596](https://sprs.parl.gov.sg/search/#/sprs3topic?reportid=written-answer-19596) — Minister Josephine Teo's Parliamentary assurance of 22 September 2025 on data preservation + criminal penalties for intentional concealment

Attachments:
- BreachBreakdown.jpg (PDPC breach-by-obligation chart: 204 Protection vs 0 Access Obligation)
- entry-gate-overlay.jpg (pdpaaccessrights.sg entry layover)
- pdpa-masking-42.png (PDPC Advisory Guidelines page 42, para 4.59: blurred/pixelated masking may not be foolproof)
- hansard.jpg (Parliament Written Answer 19596, 22 September 2025: assurance on preservation + criminal penalties)

Yours sincerely,
Lim Zirui (Ray Lim)

name: Alex Yeo
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Alex Yeo,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

- [pdpc.gov.sg/enforcement-decisions](https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/enforcement-decisions?type=Commission%27s+Decisions&page=1&sort=latest) — the official PDPC published-decisions page, filterable by obligation type

name: Mr Cai Yinzhou
subject: Yinzhou — follow-up on the PDPC grievance
body:
Dear Yinzhou,

I hope you are well. We've known each other since youth, and I am writing to you as a friend and as a member of the Government backbench.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. Previously, the filter showed a striking breakdown: across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, it had never once found a breach of the Access Obligation under section 21 of the PDPA. That pattern is now hidden from public view. I rebuilt the missing filter myself, and it remains live at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) You can verify the pattern yourself by reviewing the obligation-type column.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

To be specific about what zero looks like: PDPC aggressively enforces the Protection Obligation (section 24) against data leaks, producing dozens of published decisions including against Marina Bay Sands. The Access Obligation (section 21) has produced none. Not one published decision, across over a decade of enforcement action. This is the Access Obligation that is supposed to give Singaporeans the right to request their own data from organisations. The site at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents the full matrix, and I believe the pattern reflects a de facto policy of non-enforcement implemented through invented evidentiary standards.

name: Cassandra Lee
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Cassandra Lee,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: Chan Chun Sing
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Coordinating Minister Chan Chun Sing,

A Public Service process failure warrants your attention as Coordinating Minister for Public Services. When I brought my complaint against the Personal Data Protection Commission to the Public Service Commission, the Infocomm Media Development Authority informed them that the issues had been addressed. That representation was made at a time when no substantive reply had been provided to me. The Public Service Commission relied on IMDA’s statement and declined to act further. No remaining executive oversight channel exists outside Parliament as a result. Does IMDA’s representation to the Public Service Commission warrant review?

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This is not explained by the text of the Act. I raised this with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). As Coordinating Minister for Public Services, does IMDA’s representation to the Public Service Commission warrant review?

body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Minister Chee Hong Tat,

As Minister for National Development, your portfolio oversees the Building and Construction Authority and the Urban Redevelopment Authority, both of which interact with residents and the public through systems that collect and process personal data. The documented PDPC pattern documented at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) is directly relevant to the housing and development sector, where Town Councils and management corporations hold significant amounts of data about residents in their care. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. In my own case, CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied and then deleted while my access request was still live.

Would you consider reviewing whether the development and housing sector entities under your purview have the systems and oversight needed to honour the statutory right of access under the PDPA?

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. name: Dennis Tan Lip Fong
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Dennis Tan Lip Fong,

Your professional background as an advocate and solicitor, and your consistent engagement with civil-rights questions in this House, give you a rare toolkit for the issue I want to put before you. I write precisely because statutory interpretation is your professional vocabulary, and what I am describing is, at its core, a question of law.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its enforcement-decisions website. That filter had previously allowed segmentation by obligation type. After the redesign, no such segmentation exists. The zero Access Obligation breach findings, a statistic plainly derivable from the published record before the change, became effectively undiscoverable for a regular member of the public. The data was not deleted. It was made inconvenient.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal remains the most honest framing I can offer. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act provides individuals the right to access their personal data. PDPC has stated, in correspondence with me, that its guidelines are not determinative and that the PDPA takes precedence; yet it has found zero breaches of s.21 across 374 published enforcement actions. Section 24, by contrast, has generated active enforcement, including fines at major institutions. The inconsistency between the stated position and the enforcement record is, I submit, a matter that deserves scrutiny from someone with your background.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would you consider scrutinising PDPC's "guidelines not determinative" position from a statutory-interpretation angle?

name: Diana Pang Li Yen
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Diana Pang Li Yen,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: Mr Dinesh Vasu Dash
subject: Dinesh — follow-up on the PDPC grievance
body:
Dear Dinesh,

I hope you are well. You were my group director at MOH when I served there during Covid, and I am writing to you now in your capacity as Minister of State at the Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth, and the Ministry of Manpower. While your portfolio does not directly oversee PDPC, the principles at stake here cut across ministries.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. Previously, the filter showed a striking breakdown: across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, it had never once found a breach of the Access Obligation under section 21 of the PDPA. That pattern is now hidden from public view. I rebuilt the missing filter myself, and it remains live at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) You can verify the pattern yourself by reviewing the obligation-type column.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

To be specific about what zero looks like: PDPC aggressively enforces the Protection Obligation (section 24) against data leaks, producing dozens of published decisions including against Marina Bay Sands. The Access Obligation (section 21) has produced none. Not one published decision, across over a decade of enforcement action. As Minister of State covering two ministries, you can use your standing to ask PDPC and the GPC for Digital Development and Information why that record stands. The site at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents the full matrix, and I believe the pattern reflects a de facto policy of non-enforcement implemented through invented evidentiary standards.

I saw your commitment to evidence-based policy and public accountability during my time at MOH. I approach this matter with the same discipline: the documentary record is publicly available, and I stand ready to be corrected if any conclusion is wrong.

As someone who worked under your leadership, I would ask whether your office will review the documented pattern of how PDPC has handled access-rights cases, based solely on the public record and the evidence.

Yours sincerely,
Lim Zirui (Ray Lim)

---

You were my group director at MOH when I served there during Covid. I want to be clear: I am writing to you not because of that connection, but because the documented pattern of regulatory conduct speaks for itself. This case stands on its own merits and the publicly available record. I am not asking for any special treatment or favourable outcome. I am asking only that the record be reviewed.

name: Edwin Tong Chun Fai
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Minister Edwin Tong,

The Personal Data Protection Commission gave a reason for dismissing my access request complaint that warrants the Law Ministry’s attention. PDPC wrote that its Advisory Guidelines are not determinative and that the PDPA takes precedence. Yet in the same decisions, it used those same Guidelines as the sole basis for finding that my footage was not personal data, citing the pixelated quality of the images. It is unclear how Guidelines can simultaneously be not determinative for PDPC and yet be used as the sole basis for a finding against a complainant. This contradiction is documented at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). Has the Law Ministry reviewed PDPC’s interpretation of sections 2(1)(b) and 21?

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This is not explained by the text of the Act. I raised this with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). Has the Law Ministry reviewed PDPC’s interpretation of sections 2(1)(b) and 21?

name: Eileen Chong Pei Shan
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Ms Eileen Chong Pei Shan,

Your role as a Non-Constituency Member of Parliament gives you a particular platform to raise issues that might otherwise receive less parliamentary attention. I want to offer you one that I believe fits that description precisely, and that affects Singaporeans beyond my own case.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had allowed members of the public to segment published cases by obligation type. After the redesign, it is no longer possible to do that without specialist knowledge of the site. The zero Access Obligation breach finding, which had been empirically visible in the data, became inaccessible to most people. This was not announced; it simply changed.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the clearest analogy I know. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act establishes the right of individuals to access their data. Section 24 establishes the protection obligation. PDPC enforces s.24 actively, obtaining fines from major institutions. It has never published a breach finding under s.21 in 374 cases. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding in the entire record.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would you consider this a topic suitable for NCMP-style advocacy?

Dear Elysa Chen,

body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Senior Parliamentary Secretary Eric Chua,

As Senior Parliamentary Secretary at MOL and MSF, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

name: Gan Kim Yong
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Deputy Prime Minister Gan Kim Yong,

The documented pattern at the Personal Data Protection Commission warrants Cabinet-level attention. Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This is not a statistical artefact. It reflects a consistent interpretive position that effectively removes CCTV footage from the protection of the Act. Additionally, when I brought my complaint to the Public Service Commission, the Infocomm Media Development Authority informed them that the issues had been addressed. That representation was made at a time when no substantive reply had been provided to me. The Public Service Commission relied on it. Does the regulator’s posture toward the Access Obligation warrant Cabinet-level review?

PDPC responded to my complaint by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter that had made the disparity visible. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. Does a matter affecting a statutory right granted to all Singaporeans warrant Cabinet-level review?

Dear Mr Gerald Giam Yean Song,

Your work on civil liberties and digital rights gives you an established connection to the issue I want to raise. You have engaged with questions of data protection and individual rights before, and I hope this falls within the range of matters you are willing to examine further.

In March 2025, PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website. A previously available filter that allowed segmentation of published cases by obligation type was removed. The effect was not data loss but data obscuration: the zero Access Obligation breach finding, visible to any researcher before the redesign, became inaccessible to most members of the public after it. No announcement accompanied this change.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal illustrates the dynamic precisely. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers are straightforward. Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act establishes the Access Obligation. Section 24 establishes the Protection Obligation. PDPC has enforced s.24 actively and publicly, obtaining fines from major organisations. It has found zero breaches of s.21. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the absence in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding in the entire dataset.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

I make this representation in good faith, and I am grateful for your time.

Would you consider raising this in your areas of parliamentary focus?

name: Gho Sze Kee
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Gho Sze Kee,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

Dear Senior Parliamentary Secretary Goh Hanyan,

As Senior Parliamentary Secretary at MCCY and MSE, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. body:
Dear Members of the Government Parliamentary Committee for Digital Development and Information,

As the GPC tasked with scrutinising legislation and programmes of the Ministry of Digital Development and Information, you sit directly in the line of accountability for the regulator whose pattern is documented at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The Government Whip's office, in mooting the GPC in 1987, said its purpose was to serve as an additional channel of feedback on government policy. This email asks the GPC to exercise that function in respect of three documented findings about the Personal Data Protection Commission.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

Within the GPC's remit, would the Committee consider raising this documented gap in any upcoming MDDI-related hearing, scrutiny motion, or written-question cycle?

name: Hazlina Abdul Halim
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Hazlina Abdul Halim,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: He Ting Ru
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Ms He Ting Ru,

As one of the newer voices representing Sengkang GRC, you have an opportunity to engage with issues that affect your residents in ways that may not have received parliamentary attention yet. The issue I want to raise falls into that category, and I write in the hope that you might find it worthy of examination.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had enabled public segmentation of cases by obligation type. After the redesign, the segmentation is gone, and the zero Access Obligation breach finding, visible in the data before the change, is no longer accessible to a regular user without specialised knowledge of the site. The information was made inconvenient, not removed.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the most honest shorthand I have found for what PDPC has done. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access data an organisation holds about them. PDPC has never found a breach of that right in any of its 374 published enforcement decisions. In contrast, it actively pursues Protection Obligation cases under s.24, obtaining fines and compliance requirements. The asymmetry is documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.)

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would you consider a substantive motion or parliamentary question on behalf of residents who have found this right unenforceable in practice?

name: Indranee Rajah
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Minister Indranee Rajah,

A regulatory interpretation gap affecting the statutory Access Obligation warrants your procedural attention. Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, the Personal Data Protection Commission has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This discrepancy is not explained by the text of the statute. As Leader of the House, I am asking whether parliamentary procedure offers a legitimate avenue to raise a systemic regulatory concern of this kind.

PDPC responded to my complaint by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter that had made the disparity visible. The new layout hid the zero-Access-Obligation-breach pattern entirely. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so that the pattern is available for any MP who wishes to examine it.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so that the pattern would be publicly visible. The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. Can parliamentary procedure offer a means to raise this regulatory interpretation gap, and would a one-page summary help for any Order Paper preparation?

body:
Dear Mr Jamus Jerome Lim,

Your academic engagement with data policy and your background speaking on SME-related matters give you a particularly useful lens on what I want to raise. You have already demonstrated willingness to engage with empirical patterns in policy, and that is precisely what I am asking for here.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. The filter had allowed users to segment cases by obligation type. After the redesign, that segmentation is no longer possible. The zero Access Obligation breach findings, which had been empirically visible in the data, became inaccessible to a non-technical user. This was not explained; it was simply changed.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the clearest analogy I know. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The empirical pattern is stark. Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals a right to access their data. Section 24 imposes a protection obligation. PDPC has obtained fines and compliance undertakings under s.24, from organisations including, notably, Marina Bay Sands. It has obtained nothing under s.21 in 374 published cases. The figure is not contested; it is simply absent from the published record. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that the data remains accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

I submit this as an empirical matter, and I welcome scrutiny of the data.

Would you consider raising this empirical pattern in a parliamentary question to MDDI?

body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Senior Minister of State Janil Puthucheary,

As Senior Minister of State at MOE and MSE, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

name: Josephine Teo
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Minister Josephine Teo,

Your Written Answer 19596, delivered in Parliament on 22 September 2025 (Hansard record: https://sprs.parl.gov.sg/search/#/sprs3topic?reportid=written-answer-19596), stated that organisations must preserve data while access requests are being processed, and that criminal penalties apply for intentional concealment. Those are clear and strong assurances. In my case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied and then destroyed while my request was still alive. I am asking whether your office still stands by those assurances, given what is now documented.

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, the Personal Data Protection Commission has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. I raised this disparity with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. That filter had made the disparity publicly visible. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so others can see the pattern. The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. Given what your Written Answer 19596 states, and given the documented pattern at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg), does your office still stand by those assurances?

subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Coordinating Minister K Shanmugam,

A CCTV access loophole has direct consequences for public safety. Under the Personal Data Protection Act, organisations can deploy cameras widely and then deny access to footage by arguing it does not constitute personal data. That interpretation contradicts the statute’s plain text. It also means cameras deployed for public safety purposes can become instruments for denying accountability when footage is actually needed. The question is whether the Act is being read consistently with its purpose and with the public safety objectives it is meant to serve.

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This is not explained by the text of the Act. I raised this with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. As both Law Minister and Home Affairs Minister, does the Personal Data Protection Act read consistently with its purpose?

name: Mr Kenneth Goh
subject: Kenneth — follow-up on the PDPC grievance
body:
Dear Kenneth,

I hope this note finds you well. You were my SMU professor during my MBA, and I am writing to you in your capacity as an Associate Professor at SMU and Nominated Member of Parliament with a direct interest in regulatory interpretation and accountability.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. Previously, the filter showed a striking breakdown: across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, it had never once found a breach of the Access Obligation under section 21 of the PDPA. That pattern is now hidden from public view. I rebuilt the missing filter myself, and it remains live at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) You can verify the pattern yourself by reviewing the obligation-type column.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

To be specific about what zero looks like: PDPC aggressively enforces the Protection Obligation (section 24) against data leaks, producing dozens of published decisions including against Marina Bay Sands. The Access Obligation (section 21) has produced none. Not one published decision, across over a decade of enforcement action. This gap is not a statistical artefact; it is a consistent enforcement posture that warrants explanation. The site at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents the full matrix, and I believe the pattern reflects a de facto policy of non-enforcement implemented through invented evidentiary standards.

I studied under your teaching on legal reasoning and regulatory interpretation. I approach this matter with the same discipline: the documentary record is publicly available, and I stand ready to be corrected if any conclusion is wrong.

As an NMP with a regulatory and academic background, I would ask whether this regulatory interpretation gap, which is visible in the public record, warrants formal scrutiny through your NMP platform, based on the evidence.

- [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) — full account of the documented pattern, the rebuilt enforcement filter, and the primary record

Yours sincerely,
Lim Zirui (Ray Lim)

---

You were my SMU professor during my MBA. I want to be transparent: I am writing to you not because of that connection, but because the documented pattern of regulatory conduct speaks for itself. This case stands on its own merits and the publicly available record. I am not asking for any special treatment or favourable outcome. I am asking only that the record be reviewed.

subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Kenneth Tiong Boon Kiat,

As one of the newer Members of Parliament representing Aljunied GRC with the Workers' Party, and with your professional background spanning finance and technology, you are well placed to engage with issues that sit at the intersection of data rights, the public sector, and their practical enforcement. I write to raise one such issue that I believe deserves parliamentary attention.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had previously allowed public segmentation of cases by obligation type. After the redesign, no such segmentation exists, and the zero Access Obligation breach finding, which was empirically visible in the data before the change, became inaccessible to a regular member of the public. The information was made inconvenient, not removed.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the most direct analogy. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access their personal data. Section 24 imposes a protection obligation. PDPC has enforced s.24 actively, obtaining fines from major institutions. It has never published a breach finding under s.21 in 374 published enforcement decisions. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would your constituency office find it useful to have this documented gap on record, should a resident raise it with your team?

**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Nominated Member of Parliament Kuah Boon Theng,

As a Nominated Member of Parliament with a legal practice, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

name: Lawrence Wong
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Prime Minister Lawrence Wong,

I am writing about nineteen months of escalation through every available channel, ending without a substantive reply. Two appeals were submitted on my behalf by my Member of Parliament. Neither received a response that addressed the substance. When I approached the Public Service Commission, the Infocomm Media Development Authority informed them that the issues had been addressed. That representation was made at a time when no substantive reply had been provided to me. The Public Service Commission relied on IMDA’s statement and declined to act further. This leaves no remaining executive oversight channel outside Parliament. I am asking whether this warrants direct Prime-Ministerial review.

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, the Personal Data Protection Commission has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. I raised this with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. Given this pattern through every available channel, does the matter warrant direct Prime-Ministerial review?

name: Lee Hsien Loong
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Senior Minister Lee Hsien Loong,

The Personal Data Protection Act was enacted in 2012 under your Government. Its plain text grants a right of access to personal data, which CCTV footage pre-eminently is. The Personal Data Protection Commission’s published decisions have narrowed the definition of personal data in ways the statute does not support. This gap between what the Act says and what the regulator has done is now documented at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The question I am raising is whether the statute still means what it said when enacted.

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This is not explained by the text of the Act. I raised this with PDPC. It responded by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg).

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). The consequences of that denial have shaped every day since. Every aspect of my recovery and my life has been affected. How should the enforcement norm that Parliament established in 2012 be understood today, given what the documentary record now shows?
