name: Chua Kheng Wee Louis
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Louis Chua Kheng Wee,

As part of the Workers' Party team representing Sengkang GRC, you are well placed to engage with issues that affect residents across the constituency. The issue I want to raise is one that I believe has systemic implications beyond my own case, and I write to ask whether it might be useful material for your constituency work.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had previously allowed public segmentation of cases by obligation type. After the redesign, no such segmentation is available, and the zero Access Obligation breach finding, which was visible in the data before the change, became inaccessible to a regular member of the public. The information was made inconvenient, not removed.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the most direct analogy I know. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access their personal data. Section 24 imposes a protection obligation. PDPC has enforced s.24 actively, obtaining fines from major organisations. It has never published a breach finding under s.21 across 374 cases. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains accessible to any resident who wants to verify it. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would your constituency office find it useful to have this documented gap on record, should a resident raise it with your team?

**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Senior Minister of State Low Yen Ling,

As Senior Minister of State at MCCY and MTI, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

name: Mark Lee
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mark Lee,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: Masagos Zulkifli Bin Masagos Mohamad
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Minister Masagos Zulkifli,

As Minister for Social and Family Development, your portfolio oversees some of the most vulnerable members of society, including children and families in contact with social service agencies, and individuals receiving government support. When the statutory right of access under the PDPA is not effectively enforced, it is these communities who are least equipped to challenge refusals and most harmed when evidence they are entitled to access is denied or destroyed. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. In my own case, CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied and then deleted while my access request was still live.

Would you consider reviewing whether the social and family service agencies under your charge have the systems and oversight needed to honour the statutory right of access that every vulnerable individual holds under the PDPA?

name: Muhammad Fadli Bin Mohammed Fawzi
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Muhammad Fadli Bin Mohammed Fawzi,

As one of the newer Members of Parliament representing Aljunied GRC with the Workers' Party, you have an opportunity to engage with issues that may not yet have received full parliamentary examination. The issue I want to raise falls into that category, and I write in the hope that it might be useful material for your constituency work.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had previously allowed public segmentation of cases by obligation type. After the redesign, the segmentation is no longer available, and the zero Access Obligation breach finding, which was empirically visible in the data before the change, became inaccessible to most members of the public. The information was made inconvenient, not erased.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal is the most direct analogy. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access their personal data. Section 24 imposes a protection obligation. PDPC has enforced s.24 actively, obtaining fines from organisations that fail to protect data. It has never published a breach finding under s.21 in 374 published enforcement decisions. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the most consistent finding in the record.

Mr Lim, I have rebuilt the filter independently at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) so that this data remains publicly accessible. Every Singaporean deserves to know that the Access Obligation, a right written into law, has never produced a published enforcement outcome in the history of the PDPA.

Would your constituency office find it useful to have this documented gap on record, should a resident raise it with your team?

name: Ong Ye Kung
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Coordinating Minister Ong Ye Kung,

As Coordinating Minister for Social Policies and Health, your portfolio touches on areas directly affected by the documented PDPC pattern. The three findings below are framed for senior ministerial consideration.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. While PDPC has aggressively enforced the Protection Obligation against data breaches at major operators such as Marina Bay Sands, it has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation that every Singaporean relies on to obtain their own data. The full record is at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied and then deleted while my access request was still live.

Would you consider commissioning a formal review of PDPC's enforcement posture toward the Access Obligation, given your mandate over the health and social policy institutions affected by this pattern?

name: Pritam Singh
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Mr Pritam Singh,

Thank you for serving as the senior opposition voice in Parliament. The Workers' Party bloc has consistently engaged on PDPA reform, and your scrutiny of these matters carries particular weight in the public record.

In March 2025, PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website. A filter that previously allowed anyone to segment published cases by obligation type was removed. The effect was immediate and measurable: the pattern of zero Access Obligation breach findings, plainly visible in the data before the redesign, became invisible by design. A user visiting the site today cannot confirm what the record showed as recently as February 2025.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act gives individuals the right to access their data. PDPC has never, across its entire published enforcement record, found a breach of that obligation. In contrast, it aggressively pursues Protection Obligation cases under s.24, obtaining fines and undertakings from organisations that fail to secure data. The asymmetry is not accidental. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) documents every published case; the zero in the Access Obligation column is the only consistent finding across 374 decisions.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage was denied and then destroyed. This is not a personal grievance; it is a structural failure that affects any Singaporean who seeks to verify what an organisation holds about them.

Would the Workers' Party consider filing a substantive motion to address the documented gap in Access Obligation enforcement?

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: Seah Kian Peng
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Speaker Seah Kian Peng,

A documented regulatory interpretation gap affecting the statutory Access Obligation raises a procedural question. Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, the Personal Data Protection Commission has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation, while finding 204 breaches of the Protection Obligation in the same period. It has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including data breach decisions against major organisations. This discrepancy is not explained by the text of the statute. I am asking, as a procedural matter, whether a Member of Parliament could raise this documented gap under the substantive motion procedure. I am not asking you to rule on the substance. I am asking whether parliamentary procedure can accommodate this issue.

PDPC responded to my complaint by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter that had made the disparity visible. The new layout hid the zero-Access-Obligation-breach pattern entirely. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so that the pattern is available for any MP who wishes to examine it.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. I rebuilt the removed filter at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so that the pattern would be publicly visible. Can the substantive motion procedure be used by a Member to raise this gap, and may I offer to brief any MP who wishes to examine it?

Four sites to verify:
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) — full account of the documented pattern, the rebuilt enforcement filter, and the primary record
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) — the rebuilt enforcement matrix showing 0 Access Obligation breach findings across 374 published decisions
- [pdpc.gov.sg/enforcement-decisions](https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/enforcement-decisions?type=Commission%27s+Decisions&page=1&sort=latest) — the official PDPC published-decisions page, filterable by obligation type
- [Hansard: Written Answer 19596](https://sprs.parl.gov.sg/search/#/sprs3topic?reportid=written-answer-19596) — Minister Josephine Teo's Parliamentary assurance of 22 September 2025 on data preservation + criminal penalties for intentional concealment

Attachments:
- BreachBreakdown.jpg (PDPC breach-by-obligation chart: 204 Protection vs 0 Access Obligation)
- entry-gate-overlay.jpg (pdpaaccessrights.sg entry layover)
- pdpa-masking-42.png (PDPC Advisory Guidelines page 42, para 4.59: blurred/pixelated masking may not be foolproof)
- hansard.jpg (Parliament Written Answer 19596, 22 September 2025: assurance on preservation + criminal penalties)

Yours sincerely,
Lim Zirui (Ray Lim)

name: Shawn Huang Wei Zhong
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Senior Parliamentary Secretary Shawn Huang Wei Zhong,

As Senior Parliamentary Secretary at MOF and MOM, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

name: Sylvia Lim
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Ms Sylvia Lim,

Your long record on civil liberties and administrative law is well recognised, and your voice in these matters carries weight that few others in this House can claim. I write with that record in mind, and in the hope that it may be extended to cover a structural deficiency that has gone largely unexamined.

In March 2025, PDPC removed a filter from its published enforcement-decisions page. That filter had enabled anyone to view cases segmented by obligation type. Its removal did not delete the data; it merely made the data harder to access. The zero-Access-Obligation-breach pattern, visible to any researcher who cared to look before the redesign, became effectively hidden after it. This is a documented change to a public-facing government tool, made without any public announcement, explanation, or justification.

The Maradona Hand-of-God goal offers a framing. To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The legal framework is clear. Section 21 of the Personal Data Protection Act grants every individual a right to access their personal data. Section 24 imposes a Protection Obligation on organisations. PDPC enforces s.24 actively, obtaining fines and compliance undertakings across dozens of cases, including from major institutions. It has never found a breach of s.21 in any published decision, a finding that has remained consistent across every cycle of enforcement data. The enforcement-index at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) shows this zero across 374 cases.

I stand ready to be corrected on any factual claim I make, I invite engagement on the substance, and I thank you for your time.

Would you consider raising this in Committee of Supply?

Four sites to verify:
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) — full account of the documented pattern, the rebuilt enforcement filter, and the primary record

name: Tan Kiat How
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Senior Minister of State Tan Kiat How,

As Senior Minister of State at MDDI, you are directly in the line of accountability for the regulator whose pattern is documented at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg). I am writing to flag three findings below and to ask whether your office will review PDPC's enforcement posture toward the Access Obligation.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. While PDPC has aggressively enforced the Protection Obligation against data breaches at major operators such as Marina Bay Sands, it has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation that every Singaporean relies on to obtain their own data. The full record is at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied and then deleted while my access request was still live.

Would your office consider issuing a public statement on what this documented pattern means for the statutory right of access that every Singaporean holds under the Act?

name: Terence Ho
subject: Follow-up on my PDPC complaint, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Terence Ho,

I am writing to update you on the situation I raised with you last year regarding my complaint against PDPC.

I still do not know what happened to me on the night of the accident, because the footage that would have shown me was denied, and then destroyed.

Recently I discovered that PDPC redesigned its enforcement-decisions website so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by the type of obligation breached (PDPC's own page: https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/regulatory-guidance?page=1&sort=latest). I had previously pointed out that PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation across its entire published enforcement record. That has not changed.

In response, I rebuilt the missing filter myself: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html.) I have also published a full account of what went wrong in my case, so that PDPC cannot simply let the matter be forgotten: [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/index.html.)

Beyond my own case, PDPC has never investigated a single CCTV access-denial complaint in the entire history of its published decisions. Having seen how my own case was ultimately resolved, it is not hard to infer why that number stays at zero.

The PDPA's definition of personal data has more than one limb, in the same way a bare membership number is treated as personal data once it can be combined with other information an organisation holds. PDPC's own Advisory Guidelines (page 42, para 4.59) go further and state that even blurred or pixelated masking may not be foolproof, because individuals in such images can still be identifiable. I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

I was identified from that very same footage, on-site, within minutes. PDPC then used that same footage to conclude it was not personal data at all.

I would like to ask you directly: is it right that rights under the law can be denied this way, by the very body appointed to protect them?

name: Xie Yao Quan
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
**Subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention**

Dear Deputy Speaker Xie Yao Quan,

As Deputy Speaker of Parliament, your portfolio intersects with the same public sector institutions that the documented PDPC pattern implicates. I am writing to share three findings and to ask what you can do within your remit to help address this.

PDPC recently redesigned its enforcement-decisions page so that it is no longer possible to filter published cases by obligation type. The previous filter exposed a striking pattern: across its entire published enforcement record, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. The new layout hides that pattern entirely. Anyone who previously relied on the filter to assess PDPC's enforcement record can no longer do so.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

The numbers bear this out. Across PDPC's entire published enforcement record, documented at [https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html,) there has not been a single breach finding under the Access Obligation, even as PDPC has aggressively pursued data-breach cases under the Protection Obligation. This asymmetry between the two enforcement tracks is itself a documented pattern. In my own case, the CCTV footage that would have shown what happened to me was denied access to, and was subsequently deleted, while my statutory access request was still pending and active.

Would you consider raising this through your channels, so that this pattern can receive the parliamentary scrutiny it warrants?

name: Zhulkarnain Abdul Rahim
subject: Follow-up on the PDPC grievance, an update and a request for your attention
body:
Dear Minister of State Zhulkarnain Abdul Rahim,

You have been my constituency MP for Chua Chu Kang. Over the past months I have emailed you on this matter many times, and the record of those emails is verifiable. I am writing to put before you the full picture, because the public record alone does not show it.

The picture is this. It is clear as day that access requests were made. It is clear as day that there was no DPO provided, or the Managing Agent denied giving me the DPO. It is clear that the MA DPO also denied me my access request without valid reasons. It is clear that video was captured and deleted. And yet, PDPC found no breach.

I thank you for the parliamentary question that resulted in Written Answer 19596, delivered on 9 September 2025. In that answer, Minister Josephine Teo stated that organisations must preserve data while access requests are being processed, with criminal penalties for intentional concealment. Those are clear assurances. Since that answer, PDPC has not acknowledged any breach in my case. It continued to maintain that the footage was not personal data, a position applied inconsistently across its published decisions. Now that you are a Minister, you can no longer ask written questions on my behalf — that is the way Parliament works. I am sure your colleagues can do so, and I am hoping that someone will find the time. Unless there are more important matters that take precedent, I hope you can find an opportunity to think about why PDPC can act in this way without any recourse.

PDPC responded to my complaint by redesigning its enforcement-website to remove the obligation-type filter. The new layout hid the zero-Access-Obligation-breach pattern entirely. I rebuilt the filter and published everything at [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) so that the pattern is publicly visible.

To borrow an analogy from football: this is the Hand of God moment. I asked the referee, PDPC, to review the play. It refused to examine the evidence and let the goal stand. Today's game has VAR, cameras, and replay. Even with all that evidence available, the referee still looked away. Worse, in football that referee can be reviewed and held to account. Here, the enforcer sits in judgement of itself. Nothing happens.

But here is the further point. A referee has discretion on which marginal fouls to call â a fast game, professional judgement. But when a clear foul is committed and the offending player scores, the referee cannot simply wave play on and invent a reason. That is not discretion. That is dereliction of duty. PDPC has done that with every CCTV access-denial complaint across its entire published enforcement record.

Across over a decade of published enforcement decisions, PDPC has never once found a breach of the Access Obligation. By contrast, it has pursued Protection Obligation cases aggressively, including high-profile data breach decisions against major organisations. The footage that would have shown me what happened was denied and then destroyed. Would you consider a follow-up written question asking how many Access Obligation complaints have been filed with PDPC since 2012, and why zero breach findings have been recorded for that obligation?

Four sites to verify:
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg) — full account of the documented pattern, the rebuilt enforcement filter, and the primary record
- [pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html](https://pdpaaccessrights.sg/enforcement-index.html) — the rebuilt enforcement matrix showing 0 Access Obligation breach findings across 374 published decisions
- [pdpc.gov.sg/enforcement-decisions](https://www.pdpc.gov.sg/organisations/regulations-decisions/enforcement-decisions?type=Commission%27s+Decisions&page=1&sort=latest) — the official PDPC published-decisions page, filterable by obligation type
